---
title: "LeetCode 448. Find All Numbers Disappeared in an Array"
subtitle: "Finding the Missing Numbers"
lang: en
date: 2026-07-16
author: Anthony
categories: [LeetCode, Array]
description: "[Problem link](https://leetcode.com/problems/find-all-numbers-disappeared-in-an-array/description/)"
image: ../../cover/LeetCode_Logo_black_with_text.svg
alternate-lang: "/dsa/posts/leetcode/0448-find-disappeared-numbers/0448-find-disappeared-numbers.html"
---

Given an integer array `nums` of length $n$, where every element falls between $1$ and $n$ inclusive, some numbers may appear more than once while others may not appear at all. Find every number in the range $[1, n]$ that does not appear in `nums`, and return the result as an array. The solution should run in $\mathcal{O}(n)$ time.

For example, given the input

```
nums = [4, 3, 2, 7, 8, 2, 3, 1]
```

with length $n = 8$ and a value range of $[1, 8]$, both $5$ and $6$ are missing from the array, so the expected output is `[5, 6]`.

## Approach

The most intuitive solution is to keep track of which numbers have already appeared, then, once the traversal of `nums` is complete, check which numbers between `1` and `n` were never recorded — those are the missing values. A hash map can tally occurrences in $\mathcal{O}(n)$ time, though it also requires $\mathcal{O}(n)$ additional space.

Since every value is guaranteed to fall within `1` to `n`, a boolean array can replace the hash map without losing any performance, and the index maps directly onto the numbers in `nums`, avoiding the overhead of hashing altogether.

After discussing this with Claude, it arrived at a genuinely elegant answer: because the **range is exactly `1` to `n`**, no extra container is needed at all. While traversing `nums`, the position corresponding to each number (`abs(num) - 1`) is marked negative. Once the traversal finishes, any position that is still positive corresponds to a missing number — its index plus one. This brings the extra space down to $\mathcal{O}(1)$.

## Implementation

### Approach 1: Hash Map

Traverse `nums` to count how many times each number appears, then check which numbers between `1` and `n` were never recorded.

::: {.panel-tabset}
## C

```c
int *findDisappearedNumbers(int *nums, int numsSize, int *returnSize)
{
    int *counter = (int *)calloc(numsSize + 1, sizeof(int));
    int *res = (int *)malloc(numsSize * sizeof(int));
    int count = 0;

    for (int i = 0; i < numsSize; i++)
    {
        counter[nums[i]]++;
    }

    for (int i = 1; i <= numsSize; i++)
    {
        if (counter[i] == 0)
        {
            res[count++] = i;
        }
    }

    free(counter);
    *returnSize = count;
    return res;
}
```

## C++

```cpp
vector<int> findDisappearedNumbers(vector<int> &nums)
{
    vector<int> res;
    unordered_map<int, int> counter;

    for (size_t i = 0; i < nums.size(); i++)
    {
        counter[nums[i]]++;
    }

    for (int i = 1; i <= (int)nums.size(); i++)
    {
        if (counter.find(i) == counter.end())
        {
            res.push_back(i);
        }
    }

    return res;
}
```

## Python

```python
def findDisappearedNumbers(self, nums: list[int]) -> list[int]:
    res = []
    counter = {}

    for num in nums:
        if num not in counter:
            counter[num] = 1
        else:
            counter[num] += 1

    for i in range(1, len(nums) + 1):
        if i not in counter:
            res.append(i)

    return res
```
:::

### Approach 2: Boolean Array Marking

Same idea as Approach 1, but a boolean array replaces the hash map, with each index mapping directly onto a number, which removes the hashing overhead.

::: {.panel-tabset}
## C

```c
int *findDisappearedNumbers(int *nums, int numsSize, int *returnSize)
{
    bool *seen = calloc(numsSize + 1, sizeof(bool));
    int *res = (int *)malloc(numsSize * sizeof(int));
    int count = 0;

    for (int i = 0; i < numsSize; i++)
    {
        seen[nums[i]] = true;
    }

    for (int i = 1; i <= numsSize; i++)
    {
        if (!seen[i])
        {
            res[count++] = i;
        }
    }

    free(seen);
    *returnSize = count;
    return res;
}
```

## C++

```cpp
vector<int> findDisappearedNumbers(vector<int> &nums)
{
    vector<bool> seen(nums.size() + 1, false);
    vector<int> res;

    for (size_t i = 0; i < nums.size(); i++)
    {
        seen[nums[i]] = true;
    }

    for (int i = 1; i <= (int)nums.size(); i++)
    {
        if (!seen[i])
        {
            res.push_back(i);
        }
    }

    return res;
}
```

## Python

```python
def findDisappearedNumbers(self, nums: list[int]) -> list[int]:
    seen = [False] * (len(nums) + 1)
    res = []

    for num in nums:
        seen[num] = True

    for i in range(1, len(nums) + 1):
        if not seen[i]:
            res.append(i)

    return res
```
:::

### Approach 3: In-Place Marking

Since every value falls exactly within `1` to `n`, the values themselves can serve as indices: the corresponding position is marked negative, eliminating the need for any extra space.

::: {.panel-tabset}
## C

```c
int *findDisappearedNumbers(int *nums, int numsSize, int *returnSize)
{
    int *res = (int *)malloc(numsSize * sizeof(int));
    int count = 0;

    for (int i = 0; i < numsSize; i++)
    {
        int idx = abs(nums[i]) - 1;
        nums[idx] = -abs(nums[idx]);
    }

    for (int i = 0; i < numsSize; i++)
    {
        if (nums[i] > 0)
        {
            res[count++] = i + 1;
        }
    }

    *returnSize = count;
    return res;
}
```

## C++

```cpp
vector<int> findDisappearedNumbers(vector<int> &nums)
{
    vector<int> res;

    for (size_t i = 0; i < nums.size(); i++)
    {
        int idx = abs(nums[i]) - 1;
        nums[idx] = -abs(nums[idx]);
    }

    for (int i = 0; i < (int)nums.size(); i++)
    {
        if (nums[i] > 0)
            res.push_back(i + 1);
    }

    return res;
}
```

## Python

```python
def findDisappearedNumbers(self, nums: list[int]) -> list[int]:
    res = []
    for num in nums:
        idx = abs(num) - 1
        nums[idx] = -abs(nums[idx])

    for i in range(len(nums)):
        if nums[i] > 0:
            res.append(i + 1)

    return res
```
:::
